According to data reported by the Cuban Ministry of Interior, 2,009 Cubans were deported to the island between February 2025 and April 2026, with the highest number of deportations occurring in November 2025 (371) and February 2026 (286). Cubans made up the largest share of third-country nationals deported to Mexico in 2025, with an estimated 4,353 removals occurring between January 2025 and March 2026 (another source cites 6,000 deportations for the same time period). The US administration has also expanded restrictions on travel and visas for Cuban nationals. A new travel ban limited the entry of Cuban nationals in mid-2025. Cuba was among several countries for which the US introduced visa restrictions earlier this year. Since January 21, 2026, Cuban nationals have to post $15,000 bonds in order to apply for visas. Cuba is also among the 75 countries for which the US suspended the processing of immigrant visas, deeming their nationals likely to become reliant on public benefits.
These changes in policies have left about 600,000 Cubans in the US in legal uncertainty and risk of deportation, including 110,900 Cubans who entered the country on CHNV Parole and over 500,000 Cubans on I-220A forms (an “Order of Release on Recognizance,” a document issued by US Immigration and Customs Enforcement – ICE – or Customs and Border Protection, releasing migrants from federal immigration detention into the US under specific conditions, such as attending court hearings and regularly checking).
New destinations for Cuban migration in Latin America
In response to US restrictions on entry and legal residence for Cubans, countries across South America and Central America are increasingly becoming final destinations of Cuban mixed migration rather than mere transit corridors. This has been particularly the case for Costa Rica, Brazil and Uruguay. The factors driving the shift toward these countries as destinations vary. Costa Rica used to be a transit point on the route to the US. In light of entry restrictions to the US, many Cubans now choose Costa Rica for its relative economic stability, employment opportunities, and international protection. In January 2026, 94% of Cuban respondents surveyed by IOM’s DTM indicated Costa Rica as their intended destination. Brazil also emerged as a major new destination, largely due to its accessible, rights-based migration policies and visa-free transit options. Asylum applications by Cubans there nearly doubled between 2024 and 2025 (from 22,288 to 41,919), surpassing for the first time in a decade the number of claims made by Venezuelans in Brazil in the first half of 2025.
Uruguay has emerged due to its existing Cuban community. Cubans constitute a significant portion of Uruguay’s migrant community, accounting for 20% of the country’s foreign-born population in 2023. 14,959 Cubans arrived in 2025, compared to 5,870 in 2024. They also constitute the vast majority of asylum seekers in the country; as of mid-2025, 83% of asylum seekers in Uruguay (26,626 of 31,975) were from Cuba.
As destination countries have shifted, so have transit countries. Traditionally, Cubans flew to Nicaragua and trekked north. Nicaragua suspended visa-free entry for Cubans in February 2026, a move that analysts suggest was either a direct response to US pressure or a cautionary decision by the Nicaraguan government to avoid sanctions. As a result, Cubans began flying into Guyana to reach Brazil; others are reaching Brazil via Venezuela, which also does not require a visa. Some also stay in Guyana.
These changes are also seen in Central America. Irregular entries by Cubans into Honduras plummeted by approximately 70%, dropping from over 64,000 in 2024 to under 19,000 in 2025. At the same time, Cubans’ northbound movements have not stalled completely, as sustained transit through Guatemala throughout 2025 showed.
While not a destination of choice, Mexico has become a de facto destination for many Cuban refugees and migrants. In addition to northward-bound migrants who ended up stranded in Mexico, the country has increasingly been receiving deportations of third-country nationals, with Cubans constituting the largest group. As a result of Cubans being stranded in Mexico, the country recorded almost 40,000 asylum applications by Cuban nationals in 2025.
Migration policies and responses in destination and transit countries
As Cuban migration has shifted toward new destination countries, government responses have been mixed. When being elected, Costa Rica’s new president, Laura Fernández, promised to implement stricter migration policies by reinforcing border controls, expanding the use of tracking databases, and increasing the immigration police force. Yet, earlier this month, the Costa Rican government announced a special category to regularise thousands of Nicaraguans, Cubans, Venezuelans, and Colombians who have been stuck in a legal limbo for years while waiting for a response to their asylum requests. This measure is expected to benefit about 10,000 people, while there were about 318,000 awaiting a resolution at the start of 2026, with nearly 29,000 new asylum seekers having arrived in the country in 2025 alone.
Brazil maintains an open policy, without having made any specific adjustments for the recent arrivals. In October 2025, Brazil adopted a new National Policy on Migration, Refuge, and Statelessness (Decree No. 12,657/2025). The decree sets out the guidelines, principles, and objectives guiding the Federal Government’s actions in the areas of migration, asylum, and statelessness. The policy constitutes a rare positive development in regional migration governance, positioning Brazil as a regional model for human rights-based migration policy. At the end of 2025, Brazil also created a new humanitarian visa programme. Since the programme is open to all nationalities, some Cubans might benefit.
Uruguay offers well-established systems to support refugees and migrants’ integration, including the National Integration Plan, which was approved in 2023. However, access to formal residency remains limited: MMC’s study on integration in Montevideo found that only 20% of the 260 Cuban respondents had secured some type of temporary or permanent residence, largely due to higher costs and stricter requirements resulting from Cuba’s non-membership in Mercosur. As a result, most Cubans rely on alternative legal statuses, primarily as asylum seekers. The Uruguayan government recently created the “Residencia por Arraigo“ (Residence permit based on ties to the country) programme, offering a new pathway for migrants who arrived legally but lack residency, enabling them to settle in the country permanently. About 20,000 refugees and migrants were expected to benefit from the programme, particularly Cubans.
Mexico has entered into an “unwritten” or informal agreement with the US to act as a de facto safe third country, accepting the deportation of thousands of third-country nationals, including Cubans. The conditions of this deal are unclear, as the Mexican President, Claudia Sheinbaum, has denied the existence of an agreement and has cited humanitarian reasons for accepting deportees; yet, pressure from the US most likely plays a role.
Mexican authorities typically receive deportees at northern border points, from where they are transported to southern cities like Tapachula (Chiapas) and Villahermosa (Tabasco). Unlike Mexican citizens who are deported, the Mexican government provides no reception infrastructure, shelter, or financial assistance for third-country deportees. Cubans are often released into the streets with little to no orientation. Furthermore, while Mexican authorities immediately expel other migrants across the southern border to Guatemala or Honduras, Cubans are simply released into Mexico. For Cubans, the only option to regularise their status in Mexico is to apply for asylum. Yet, access to asylum is currently hampered by funding-related backlogs, the introduction of additional requirements like digital pre-registration, and the de facto suspension of the Humanitarian Visitor Card (TVRH), which previously offered temporary legal status and work access.
Challenges for Cuban refugees and migrants
The changes in US policies have various implications for Cuban refugees and migrants, both those in transit and those who previously settled in the US.
Between 2019 and 2023, approximately 500,000 Cubans were released into the US with an I-220A form rather than a humanitarian parole. This arbitrary bureaucratic distinction prevents them from adjusting their status to obtain permanent residency under the Cuban Adjustment Act and makes them subject to deportation. Deported Cubans reported inhumane conditions in ICE detention centres, including overcrowding, insufficient food, contaminated water, solitary confinement, physical and verbal abuse and a lack of medical care or information about their cases. Between late 2025 and early 2026, the US also transferred some Cubans to the maximum-security military prison at the Guantánamo Bay naval base, with deportees thinking they were being deported to Cuba. They were held at Guantánamo for weeks in severe distress before eventually being sent back to the US and deported to Cuba.
In Mexico, Cuban deportees are stranded in cities like Tapachula and Villahermosa with virtually no access to shelter, healthcare, or work. Many report sleeping on the streets, going without medication, and being exposed to cartel violence. They are facing a legal limbo due to their lack of legal status in Mexico and an absence of Cuban consular support. The government lacks a presence in the south, and the Cuban embassy in Mexico City has reportedly been unresponsive to migrants’ requests. A recent report by Human Rights Watch documented the conditions faced by Cubans deported to cities in southern Mexico, finding that their options to regularise their stay are largely limited to applying for refugee status. However, accessing this system is made harder as deportees lack phones or email accounts, and face delays in obtaining appointments as well as practical difficulties in meeting requirements like periodic check-ins with authorities.
As more Cubans settle in South American countries like Uruguay, they may also face challenges to their integration in areas such as housing, employment, and consular support. In Montevideo, newly arrived migrants are effectively shut out of the formal rental market due to strict guarantee requirements, pushing them into overcrowded, precarious living conditions. Bureaucratic delays in revalidating foreign qualifications force many into informal, low-paying work. While other refugees and migrants face similar challenges, the Cuban government offers little to no consular support. Finally, increased US pressure on Cuba is exacerbating an already dire economic crisis, resulting in severe shortages of food, medicine, and basic necessities and continued blackouts on the island. If US pressure continues and domestic conditions deteriorate, Cuba could plunge into a major humanitarian crisis, which would be expected to drive new, large-scale migration movements throughout the Americas. This would most likely worsen conditions for Cuban migrants in host and transit countries, and force more to rely on irregular and unsafe routes.
Conclusion: Recognising and responding to the new nature of Cuban mixed migration
The reconfiguration of Cuban mixed migration in the Americas reflects a deepening humanitarian crisis driven by the near-total closure of legal pathways to the US and the absence of adequate protection frameworks in transit and destination countries. Cubans deported to Mexico face inhumane conditions and challenges to regularise their status, while those settling in South American countries are likely to encounter barriers to integration.
Addressing these challenges requires coordinated action at the regional level to ensure a shared, rights-based response. Destination and transit countries should strengthen their asylum and refugee systems to ensure Cuban refugees and migrants have effective access to protection. National and regional bodies should press Cuba to fulfil its consular obligations toward its nationals abroad.
Charlotte Müller
Charlotte Müller is an external consultant and migration expert with Diversa, an AI and data services organisation working at the intersection of data, AI, and applied social research.

items.creator: Pan American Health Organization
items.title: Mexico: Situation Report #6: Measles in the Americas Region. 2 July 2026
items.author: Pan American Health Organization
items.content:
Countries: Mexico, Belize, Bolivia (Plurinational State of), Canada, Colombia, Guatemala, Honduras, Panama, Peru, United States of America
Source: Pan American Health Organization

Please refer to the attached file.

Between epidemiological week (EW) 1 and EW 25 of 2026(ending on 27June 2026), the Region of the Americas reported 22,974 confirmed measles cases from 17 countries and territories, representing an 181% increase compared to the same period in 2025. Mexico (11,820), Guatemala (7,067), the United States (2,134) and Canada (1,079) accounted for the majority (96%) of confirmed cases (Map 1). Additionally, 39 deaths have been reported (1).

During EW 23 and EW 24 of 2026, the Region of the Americas reported 479 new confirmed measles cases across seven countries and territories, representing a 51.1% decrease compared with the previous two-week period (EW 21-22; 980 new cases) (Figure 1). This decline was driven primarily by continued decreases in Mexico, Peru, and the United States. In Canada, case numbers have remained low and appear to be plateauing, with only a small number of cases reported each week. Trends in Guatemala should be interpreted with caution, given possible reporting delays or updates in recent weeks2.

In the context of the recent earthquakes in Venezuela, the risk of measles transmission remains a concern due to suboptimal vaccination coverage, surveillance gaps, and increased population movement related to emergency response operations. Available information indicates low coverage for both first and second doses of measles-containing vaccine, as well as the presence of silent municipalities. Given the risk of possible imported cases, particularly in areas receiving humanitarian personnel, supplies, or population movements, strengthening early detection, laboratory confirmation capacity, active case finding, and vaccination of susceptible populations remains a priority to reduce the risk of importation and onward transmission.

items.creator: Mixed Migration Centre
items.title: Mauritania: Aperçu MMC Afrique du Nord et de l’Ouest 4Mi – Dynamique du trafic de migrant∙e∙s à l’intérieur et vers la Mauritanie (Juin 2026)
items.author: Mixed Migration Centre
items.content:
Country: Mauritania
Source: Mixed Migration Centre

Please refer to the attached file.

À partir des données 4Mi du MMC, ce snapshot analyse les services des passeurs1 et les interactions entre migrant∙e∙s et passeurs en Mauritanie, ainsi que le long de la route atlantique au sens large.2 Il fournit des données sur la manière dont ces services sont utilisés et vécus le long des voies terrestres, contribuant ainsi à l’élaboration de réponses politiques et programmatiques visant à renforcer la protection des personnes en mobilité.

Principales conclusions

• Le recours aux passeurs est souvent une composante anticipée de la migration vers la Mauritanie : 65% des personnes interrogées avaient déjà prévu de faire appel à un passeur avant leur départ.

• Bien que souvent prévus à l’avance, les services de passeurs sont généralement organisés à des étapes spécifiques, en particulier aux points de passage frontaliers, plutôt que pour l’ensemble du trajet. Dans l’ensemble, 70% des personnes interrogées ont fait appel à des passeurs pour une seule étape, et 80% l’ont fait spécifiquement pour entrer en Mauritanie.

• Même là où des accords d’exemption de visa existent, les personnes interrogées originaires du Sénégal et du Mali ont fait appel à des passeurs pour les aider à entrer en Mauritanie.

• Les services de passeurs vont au-delà de la simple facilitation du passage des frontières : 60% des personnes interrogées ont indiqué que les passeurs leur avaient fourni plusieurs services, notamment le transport transfrontalier (64%), l’aide à l’obtention de documents de voyage (44%), le transport à l’intérieur des pays (40%) et la facilitation des interactions avec les autorités (34%).

• Le contact avec les passeurs est le plus souvent initié par les migrant∙e∙s et les réfugié∙e∙s eux/elles-mêmes : 41% ont contacté les passeurs directement, contre 28% qui ont été approché∙e∙s par les passeurs.

• Les arrangements avec les passeurs sont souvent facilités par des réseaux personnels de confiance : 30% des personnes interrogées ont indiqué que des proches ou des amis avaient facilité le contact avec les passeurs, tandis que les recommandations de la diaspora (40%) et les conseils de la famille ou des ami∙e∙s dans le pays de départ (37%) constituaient des raisons courantes de recourir aux services de passeurs.

• Les passeurs sont considérés comme des sources d’information fiables : 52% des personnes interrogées ont reçu des informations de leur part et 32% les ont identifiés comme leur source la plus fiable.

• Les passeurs sont souvent perçus comme des prestataires de services efficaces plutôt que comme des criminels ; 71% des personnes interrogées ont déclaré qu’ils les avaient aidées à atteindre leurs objectifs de migration et 77% les ont décrits comme des entrepreneurs. Seules 3% les considéraient comme des criminels.

items.creator: Mixed Migration Centre
items.title: MMC West and North Africa 4Mi Snapshot – Smuggling dynamics within and to Mauritania (June 2026)
items.author: Mixed Migration Centre
items.content:
Country: Mauritania
Source: Mixed Migration Centre

Please refer to the attached file.

This snapshot analyses smuggling services and migrant-smuggler dynamics in Mauritania and along the broader Atlantic migration route, using MMC 4Mi data. It provides evidence on how these services are used and experienced along land routes, informing policy and programmatic responses that strengthen the protection of people on the move.

Key findings

  • Smuggling is often an anticipated component of migration to Mauritania: 65% of respondents had already planned to use a smuggler before departure.
  • Although often anticipated in advance, smuggling services are commonly arranged at specific stages, particularly at border crossings, rather than across the full journey. Overall, 70% of respondents relied on smugglers for only one segment, and 80% did so specifically to cross into Mauritania.
  • Even where visa-free arrangements exist, Senegalese and Malian respondents hired smugglers to help them cross into Mauritania.
  • Smuggling arrangements extend beyond border facilitation: 60% of respondents reported that smugglers provided multiple services, including transportation across borders (64%), support in obtaining travel documents (44%), in-country transportation (40%), and facilitation of interactions with authorities (34%).
  • Smugglers are often perceived as effective service providers rather than criminals: with 71% reporting they helped achieve their migration objectives and 77% describing them as businesspeople. Only 3% viewed them as criminals.

Source: Pacific Disaster Center

Please refer to the attached file.

The response is transitioning from live rescue to recovery. Live rescues are now rare exceptions. Missing-persons estimates range from tens of thousands (unverified crowd-sourced registries cite 43,000 to 46,000), against no confirmed official figure. The UN is planning against a scenario of up to 10,000 deaths.

items.creator: Radio Ergo
items.title: Somalia: Radio Ergo Weekly Feedback Report, Issued: 3 July 2026
items.author: Radio Ergo
items.content:
Country: Somalia
Source: Radio Ergo

Please refer to the attached file.

Brief Analysis

Many callers to Radio Ergo’s audience feedback platform in the last week of June wanted to engage with the radio’s farming and livestock experts, to overcome challenges in controlling crop pests or diseases and to access advice and treatment for livestock diseases. The largest number of calls came from people citing beneficial rainfall, some of it heavy, mostly in central and southern regions. Two callers from Hiran and Lower Juba described healthy crops ready for harvest. At the same time, a smaller number of people called about the severe impact of drought that continued to kill livestock and had left them destitute. These callers were mainly from central locations with a couple from the north. Other topics of interest for callers in this period were mental health challenges, and malnutrition. The following summarises the calls by theme.

Drought – a caller in Bali-hawd, Somaliland, said the pastoralists were grappling with drought conditions and their livestock were thin and dying off. A caller in Galkayo, Mudug, asked for aid to cope with the drought. In Galgadud region, a caller in Balanbal said they hadn’t had rainfall and their livestock continued to die because of drought. Another in Guriel said they hadn’t had rain recently and prayed for relief. A female caller in El-dhere said they faced severe drought and water shortage and appealed for aid. A female caller in Balad, M. Shabelle, said they were affected by drought and conflict. A caller in Gaal-leefeed said they were appealing for help due to the drought and water shortages.

Food Security – a caller in Jamame, L. Juba, said the farmers were happily growing their vegetables and grains. A caller in Hiran region said they were doing well and their crops were healthy. He said they had enough water and their crops were nearing the time for harvest.

Mental health – several callers engaged with the radio’s weekly “Mind” programme, talking about challenges with depression, self-talk and sleep walking, and forgetfulness. Radio Ergo’s reporting as well as the listeners’ engagement shows considerable public interest and growing awareness around mental health.

Health – a caller asked experts on the radio doctor programme to use Somali language instead of foreign terms when describing symptoms and treatments.

Nutrition – a woman in Abudwak wanted advice from the nutrition experts about how to increase her breast milk. Another in Dhaharey wanted advice on how to treat her thin, malnourished children.

Governance – a caller in Abudwak wanted the government to create jobs to ease unemployment. Another in Burdhubo, Gedo region, called on authorities to build a bridge for people and livestock to cross the river.

Livestock – a large number of callers from across Galmudug spoke of goat diseases causing deaths, with symptoms of diarrhoea, runny nose, fever, mouth sores.

Agriculture – a large number of farmers called with questions about control of insects, caterpillars, and mould damaging their crops and vegetables. Callers in Galgadud complained of drying out or burning of their crops before maturing. A caller in Jowhar wanted to know if adding kerosene to the irrigation water would help with pest control.

Rainfall – in Galgadud, once caller in Guriel said they had rainfall, whilst another said that it was not widespread and some areas remained dry. A number of callers across Hiran region spoke of rainfall, some of it very heavy. Some said farms were doing well and that river flooding had been limited. A caller in Jalalaqsi said the vegetation and grazing were growing back, although they were concerned about conflict in the area. Several in Lower and M. Shabelle spoke of beneficial rainfall helping recovery. Several in parts of Bay region including Burhakaba and Baidoa said crops and livestock were doing well with the rain. One in Gonyale, however, said this year’s rainfall had been less than expected. In Gedo region, callers in Bardera and Dollow described heavy rainfall and crops doing well. Callers in Kismayo also said rain was helping the crops and livestock.

Shabelle spoke of beneficial rainfall helping recovery. Several in parts of Bay region including Burhakaba and Baidoa said crops and livestock were doing well with the rain. One in Gonyale, however, said this year’s rainfall had been less than expected. In Gedo region, callers in Bardera and Dollow described heavy rainfall and crops doing well. Callers in Kismayo also said rain was helping the crops and livestock.

This is a guidance note on how to report funding information on ActivityInfo. Please consider the following before you begin:

• All recipients of funds should report. Partners should report all funding including projects funded by UN agencies, International NGOs and National NGOs.

• For consortia, each member should report on the portion of funding their organisation has received.

• A reminder will be sent on a quarterly basis to partners at the beginning of each reporting cycle.

• Systematic reporting on the funding situation of partners is a requirement for both appealing and non-appealing partners in the Uganda Country Refugee Response Plan (UCRRP).

• When reporting on funding received in a currency other than USD, please use the prevailing exchange rate at the time of receipt (or the rate set by your donor, if different).

PERFORMANCE TRACKING OF THE 2026-2030 UGANDA COUNTRY RRP

Introduction

An enhanced results framework has been established to track the performance of the UCRRP against indicators and to ensure progress against the targets set by partners at the start of the planning process. The ultimate goal of deploying a robust tracking framework is ensuring transparency and accountability, avoiding duplications and re-adjusting the response when inefficacies and gaps are identified. Sector Co-leads, UNHCR and OPM will regularly reach out to UCRRP partners to gather information and data for UCRRP progress reports, including through sector activity tracking. Systematic progress reporting against the indicators from the UCRRP results framework selected by the sector leads will be a requirement for all UCRRP partners (including implementing partners of UN agencies). ActivityInfo is open-source software for data collection and reporting which works both online and offline. It is optimised for reporting on activities that are geographically dispersed and implemented by multiple partner organisations. The software has been used to track activities implemented by humanitarian partners working in the Uganda refugee response since early 2019 and is offered as a service by BeDataDriven. UNHCR will provide access to this software for all partners in the response.
